ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sambal terasi. FOODON_03311737 (chili sauce food product) is the closest parent category covering chilli-based condiment pastes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers chilli sauces and similar condiment products; used as closest parent as no specific sambal terasi code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for sambal terasi or shrimp paste chilli condiment at time of processing.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sambal terasi as a condiment or side is approximately 30 g (about 2 tablespoons), as used alongside rice dishes or grilled proteins.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal Terasi (Shrimp Paste Chilli Condiment) — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient components: red chilli peppers (FDC ID 170108), shrimp paste/fermented shrimp (FDC ID 175180), shallots (FDC ID 170501), garlic (FDC ID 169230), and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian condiment analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / FAO INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for sambal terasi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted ingredient composition: approximately 50% red/bird's eye chilli, 15% shrimp paste (terasi), 15% shallots, 10% garlic, 5% lime juice, 5% sugar/salt. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for individual ingredients. FAO/INFOODS SEA data used for cross-validation of Southeast Asian chilli condiment profiles. Values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for a typical sambal terasi preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chilli-based condiments and analogous Southeast Asian sambal-type sauces — analogous estimate based on chilli pepper and mixed vegetable condiment GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal terasi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on the dominant ingredient profile: red chilli peppers and shallots have low GI values (chilli peppers GI ~23, shallots/onions GI ~10–15 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar allium and capsicum foods). The small amount of added sugar and the high fibre and water content of the preparation support a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (32 × 13.5 × 0.30) / 100 = 1.3 g, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fermented shrimp paste (terasi)
hypertension.guidance: Sambal terasi contains approximately 980 mg sodium per 100 g due to the fermented shrimp paste base; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 1 teaspoon (approximately 10 g) per meal to keep sodium contribution below 100 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and small typical serving size
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal terasi has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of only 1.3 g per 30 g serving, making it a suitable condiment for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when used in typical condiment quantities; however, added sugar in some preparations should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and cholesterol from shrimp paste; beneficial capsaicin content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (approximately 980 mg per 100 g) from fermented shrimp paste poses a cardiovascular risk if consumed in large quantities; however, capsaicin from chilli peppers may have vasodilatory benefits, and the low saturated fat content (0.7 g per 100 g) is favourable — portion control to 10–15 g per serving is recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal terasi (also known as shrimp paste chilli sambal or sambal belacan in Malay-speaking contexts) is a traditional Indonesian chilli condiment made by grinding or blending fresh red chillies and bird's eye chillies with fermented shrimp paste (terasi or belacan), shallots, garlic, lime juice, salt, and a small amount of sugar. The result is a pungent, intensely flavoured paste with a complex savoury depth derived from the umami-rich fermented shrimp paste, balanced by the heat of the chillies and the brightness of lime. The texture ranges from coarsely ground to smooth depending on preparation style. Sambal terasi is a staple condiment in Indonesian, Malaysian, and Singaporean cuisines, typically served alongside rice dishes, grilled fish, fried chicken, or vegetables. Nutritionally, it is relatively low in calories (approximately 95 kcal per 100 g) and provides modest protein (4.5 g) from the shrimp paste, with a low glycemic index of approximately 32. The primary nutritional concern is its high sodium content (approximately 980 mg per 100 g) due to the fermented shrimp paste, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is a good source of vitamin C (48 mg per 100 g) from the chilli peppers and contains beneficial capsaicin compounds. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. In Singapore, it is commonly available as a side condiment at Indonesian and Malay food stalls and restaurants.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in sambal terasi?
What is the difference between sambal terasi and sambal belacan?
What are the ingredients in sambal terasi?
How many calories in sambal terasi?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for sambal terasi as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases at the time of processing. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components (red chilli, shrimp paste, shallots, garlic). The GI value is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for chilli peppers and allium vegetables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ingredient composition (approximately 50% red/bird's eye chilli, 15% shrimp paste/terasi, 15% shallots, 10% garlic, 5% lime juice, 5% sugar and salt) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data; no single approved-source composite entry was found. 2. Sodium content is high due to fermented shrimp paste; actual sodium may vary significantly between recipes and commercial preparations. 3. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on chilli pepper and allium vegetable GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for sambal terasi was found. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients of this condiment. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for ingredient-level estimation. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant and seafood-based condiment with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. 7. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Fish and seafood' (as in the input) to 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' as sambal terasi functions as a condiment/condiment paste rather than a primary seafood dish. 8. The input food_name 'Lv.1 Shrimp Paste Chilli (Sambal Terasi)' has been canonicalised to 'Shrimp Paste Chilli Sambal (Sambal Terasi)'; the 'Lv.1' prefix is a restaurant-specific spice-level label and is not part of the canonical food name. 9. The field survey data source and restaurant data are preserved as provenance context but are not used as primary nutritional evidence per Project Veritas source policy.

Estimated nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient components as no composite approved-source entry was found.
